fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This fund holds a mix of great Indian large companies and a bit of international stocks. It’s less likely to swing wildly than pure small-cap funds, which fits an aggressive plan that still tries to protect some downside. Pick-up state-of-the-art tech and “value at fair prices” strategy.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Small-cap stocks can run away with your portfolio. Historically they have given the highest returns over long periods, but you must be ready to see big drops. This has been one of the best small-cap performers over the last 15 years.

fund.name: quant Small Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: quant is famous for hunting mid and small stocks that no one else is looking at and are sometimes mispriced. If history repeats itself, this should help your portfolio grow fast. This adds a second small-cap perspective to reduce the risk of putting all eggs in one basket.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This is a passively managed fund that just buys the 150 biggest mid-sized companies. It’s cheap (0.20% yearly fee) and doesn’t rely on one fund manager doing the right picks all the time. Over 6 years, midcaps in India have done extremely well.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This Aggressive Hybrid fund holds mostly stocks (around 65%) but also keeps some safer bonds. It’s less bumpy than pure equity funds. Since you’re in the 30% tax slab, this is taxed like an equity fund (low tax on gains).

fund.name: Invesco India Largecap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Large-caps are the stable ones. They rarely go belly-up because they are giant companies with lots of cash. This fund has very high 3- and 5-year returns. It anchors your portfolio so it doesn’t crash too hard when the market turns.

Gold doesn’t grow too much on its own, but it goes up when hedging against currency weakness or geopolitical trouble. This is about preserving your ₹20Cr target rather than making extra profit, and it acts as insurance against unexpected market events.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Check your bank for it if you haven’t already. If you need to set up the account for mutual fund investments, sign up for a free direct account on Kuvera, Groww, Coin by Zerodha, or MFU. Step 2: In one sitting, set up the SIPs for all 7 funds. Most platforms let you schedule, amount, and fund. Step 3: Keep investing every month without letting it lapse. Step 4: All these are Direct plans so you want to make sure you're not paying extra charges. Step 5: Save the AMFI codes above for your records.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: call your brokerage and make sure the SIPs are running and the money is actually being invested. Also, check your bank account to see if any of the SIPs were delayed. If so, contact support immediately.

After 1 year: look at the overall portfolio. It’s common for small-cap funds to drop 20-30% in a bad year. Don’t panic, just check if the returns are in line with the category. If one fund is lagging badly compared to others in its category, you can change or switch the SIP to that one.
Every year: as your salary increases, increase the SIP amount by about 10% (‘step up’ this monthly investment). This will help your compounding work faster.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, calculate the value of each fund. If small-caps or mid-caps have grown to 35% of your total portfolio when they should only be 20-25%, sell a bit of them and buy more of the safer options (large caps or hybrid). Conversely, if a fund has underperformed, trim it and move to the category that has consistently hit its target. Keep an eye on your allocation each year to stay in your risk zone.
plan.rationale: You are 36 with 32 years ahead, which is a rare privilege for compounding. An aggressive approach makes sense: you can afford to take big swings now because you have decades to recover. We put 45% into small and mid-caps because they offer the highest growth potential over long horizons. We added 20% in Aggressive Hybrid funds to smooth out the ride a bit while still staying mostly in stocks. The 25% in Parag Parikh Flexi gives you access to top large companies plus some international stocks, which can be a stabilizer. The 10% in Gold and Large Caps is just there to prevent your portfolio from going belly-up in a total market crash. Since you are in the 30% tax bracket, we kept all equity-heavy funds, which means gains are taxed at 12.5% after 1 year (LTCG), saving you compared to debt funds or liquid funds.
risks: Market volatility: Even strong funds can drop 20-40% in a single bad year. Small-caps in this plan have the highest chance of big drops. This is also where they are most volatile.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
Passive Instrument Fraction: 0.6 — The equity allocation (Parag Parikh Flexi Cap 25%, PP 500 unknown but dominant, Nippon Small Cap 20%, Quant Small Cap 10%, Motilal Midcap 15%, ICI Large Cap 10%) totals 65% equity. Of this, Parag Parikh and ICI are active styles, while Motilal Midcap is a pure index fund. Assuming PP 500 is passive and PP 500 is ~50% of equity, roughly 30% passive + 10% index = 45% passive in equity, plus the gold ETF (10% total, not equity) might make it a bit higher. Given the active core (PP, Nippon, Quant, ICI), 60% is a realistic estimate. 

Turnover Score: 0.8 — The plan emphasizes a long-term view (32 years of compounding), annual rebalancing only, and step-up SIPs. No short-term tactical shifts. 

Cost Emphasis Score: 0.65 — The plan explicitly mentions Direct Plans to save charges and notes the low fee of the index fund. While growth is prioritized, cost efficiency via direct routing is a stated goal. 

Research vs Cost Score: 0.45 — The plan references specific fund philosophies (e.g., "hunting mispriced stocks" for Quant, "value at fair prices" for Parag Parikh), implying reliance on active management research rather than broad-market indexing. 

Time Horizon Alignment Score: 0.9 — The strategy is built around a 32-year horizon (age 36), emphasizing compounding and long-term category growth, with no short-term trading tactics. 

Portfolio Activeness Score: 0.58 — The portfolio is heavily tilted toward active managers (Parag Parikh, Nippon Small Cap, Quant Small Cap, Invesco Large Cap) which typically exhibit higher tracking error and alpha. The addition of one index fund (Motilal Midcap) and the hybrid fund dilutes this somewhat, but the core equity exposure is actively managed.

pqs.reasoning: The plan excels in goals, risk, consistency, and tax efficiency. goal_alignment is perfect as it leverages the 32-year horizon for high risk taking. risk_return_appropriateness matches the aggressive appetite with a heavy bias toward equities/small/midcaps. internal_consistency is strong with clear rationale matching the allocation. tax_efficiency is optimized for a 30% slab investor by ensuring >65% equity exposure in hybrids and avoiding slab-taxed debt instruments. diversification is excellent geographically and sectorally; deducting points only slightly because allocating two separate small-cap funds (Nippon and Quant) together with a mid-cap index creates significant concentration in small/mid value, leaving 0% allocation to pure large-cap direct funds despite one large-cap fund in the PPF Flexi portfolio.
